preFix.title: Calculating off-grid electrical load requirements
preFix.subtitle: Sizing a battery bank and solar array from your actual daily consumption
preFix.excerpt: An off-grid electrical system must be sized to cover the worst-case daily energy demand, not the average, because a battery bank running short on a January week of low sun and high demand is the design failure condition. A systematic load calculation worksheet identifies each appliance, its wattage, and its daily hours of use, to produce a realistic daily energy demand figure that drives the battery and solar array sizing.
preFix.sourceNotes: Victron Energy off-grid system design guide 2024. Rolls Battery off-grid sizing documentation. Paul Wheaton, Off-Grid Solar (2019). Renewable UK off-grid residential case studies.

content.text: Sizing an off-grid system for average demand gives a system that runs short in bad weather. The right approach is to size for peak demand over 2 to 3 days of low sun in a row (the autonomy period). In a UK location, this means planning for winter conditions with a run of overcast days.
Step 1: list all loads
For each item, write down three things: the wattage, how many hours it runs per day in winter, and whether it can be switched off in bad weather.
Common off-grid loads and approximate wattages:
LED lighting (per room): 5 to 15 W; 4 to 6 hours/day = 20 to 90 Wh/day
Laptop: 45 to 65 W; 8 hours/day = 360 to 520 Wh/day
Refrigerator (12 V, high-efficiency): 35 to 50 W average running; continuous = 840 to 1200 Wh/day
Phone charging: 5 to 18 W; 2 hours/day = 10 to 36 Wh/day
Water pump (12 V, low-flow): 60 W; 0.5 hours/day = 30 Wh/day
Step 2: total daily demand

Sum the Wh/day figures for all loads. Add a 15% inefficiency margin for inverter and wiring losses. This gives the design daily demand in Wh. A simple off-grid cabin with essential-only loads (lights, laptop, phone, small fridge) typically runs at 1,500 to 2,500 Wh/day.
Step 3: battery bank sizing
Battery capacity (kWh) = Daily demand (kWh) × Autonomy days ÷ Max
depth of discharge
For a UK cabin with 2 kWh/day demand, 3 days of autonomy (a standard UK winter design), and lithium iron phosphate batteries at 80% DoD: capacity = 2.0 × 3 ÷ 0.80 = 7.5 kWh.
Step 4: solar array sizing
Array size (kWp) = Daily demand (kWh) ÷
peak sun hours
(winter) ÷ System efficiency (typically 0.8)

For 2 kWh/day in Yorkshire, winter 2.0 peak sun hours: 2.0 ÷ 2.0 ÷ 0.8 = 1.25 kWp minimum. In practice, size at 1.5× minimum (1.9 kWp) to allow for panel degradation and sub-optimal orientation. At 400 W per panel, that is 5 panels.
